Search results: 



--- SOURCE 1: Recent advances in biomaterials for tissue-engineered constructs ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228

SUMMARY:
Designing scaffolds that can regulate cell behavior, including proliferation and differentiation, is a crucial aspect of tissue engineering scaffold fabrication. This review paper emphasizes the critical role of stimuli delivered by scaffolds to cells and focuses on recent advances in the development of artificial scaffolds for tissue engineering. By highlighting these key developments, this review underscores the importance of scaffold design in promoting optimal cellular behavior and advancing the field of tissue engineering. This review highlights recent progress in the development of these composite biomaterials, emphasizing biomimetic design, synthesis methodologies, and applications in drug delivery, cancer therapy, tissue engineering, wound healing, antimicrobial activity, food safety, natural bio-adhesives, and various industrial uses, alongside their ecological balance on Earth within a sustainable vision.

FULL CONTENT:
Recent advances in biomaterials for tissue-engineered constructs: Essential factors and engineering techniques - ScienceDirect

Recent advances in biomaterials for tissue-engineered constructs: Essential factors and engineering techniques
==============================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Shiva Norouzi a 1, Nikoo Saveh Shemshaki b 1, Ehsan Norouzi c, Masoud Latifi d, Bahareh Azimi e, Serena Danti e f, Xiaolan Qiao g, Yuee Miao g, Shengyuan Yang g, Mohsen Gorji h, Vasilije Petrovic i, M. Ali Aboudzadeh k, Roohollah Bagherzadeh j l

Highlights
----------

*   •Tissue engineering tackles organ shortage via implantable constructs, addressing critical healthcare challenges. 
*   •It is vital in tissue engineering to design structures considering biomaterial-cell interaction, topography and morphology. 
*   •Recent advances in artificial scaffold development reveal innovative approaches, expanding their frontiers and effectiveness. 

Abstract
--------

Tissue-engineered constructs can replicate the structural and physiological properties of natural tissues. The constructs can be designed to address the transplantation issues affected by the shortage of donor tissues and organs. One of the major concerns in [tissue engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/tissue-engineering "Learn more about tissue engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is the design and development of structures that improve the interaction between materials and cells and provide an ideal platform for cells to form functional tissue. Several contributing factors need to be considered to design and fabricate the constructs, including biomaterials, biological, topographical, biophysical, and morphological factors either alone or in combination. Here, we review the application, advancement, and future directions of these essential factors in designing and developing constructs for [tissue regeneration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/tissue-regeneration "Learn more about tissue regeneration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In particular, we focus on original approaches and engineering tools to design construct parameters in tissue engineering.

Keywords
--------

Tissue engineering

Regenerative therapy

Biomimetic construct

Scaffolds

1. Introduction
---------------

In recent years, [tissue engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/tissue-engineering "Learn more about tissue engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") constructs have been considered a promising approach for the functional engineering of tissues due to the limited capabilities of the human body to regenerate its organs [[[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib2), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib3)]. [Tissue regeneration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/tissue-regeneration "Learn more about Tissue regeneration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") approaches for replacing or repairing damaged tissues consist of cell therapy, growth factors, and [biomaterial scaffolds](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biomaterial-scaffold "Learn more about biomaterial scaffolds from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), either alone or in combination. The term “tissue engineering” as recognized today, was first introduced at a National Science Foundation panel meetingn in 1987. Tissue engineering as a medical field started in the early nineties when tissue engineering was defined as “an interdisciplinary field that applies the principles of engineering and life sciences toward the development of biological substitutes that restore, maintain or improve tissue function” [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib4)]. Whereas cell therapy involves the delivery of sole cells through systemic or local injection into the bloodstream or via direct transplantation io a local tissue, the tissue engineering paradigm broadly intersects the “regenerative medicine” as later defined in 1992 [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib5)], but it is inherently founded upon three pillarsso-calleded “Tissue engineering triad” namely, cells, biomaterial scaffolds, and stimuli. Each of the three is fundamental. The matrix or scaffold helps the living cells and guides the development and regeneration of new living tissue. The scaffold can be a natural or synthetic origin, composite, or a mixture of both. The living cells can be seeded on the scaffold before implantation or can migrate into the [acellular scaffold](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/acellular-scaffold "Learn more about acellular scaffold from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") after implantation in the body. In any case, the cell behavior will be influenced by the microenvironmental stimuli induced by the scaffold.

The simplest strategy is to replace tissue with an analogous counterpart of identical or different origin. Natural tissue scaffolds, includinge [autograft](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/autograft "Learn more about autograft from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [allograft](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/allograft "Learn more about allograft from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [xenograft](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/xenograft "Learn more about xenograft from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and decellularized [extracellular matrix](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/extracellular-matrix "Learn more about extracellular matrix from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ECM), are considered current standard treatments for healing large and massive [injuries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/injury "Learn more about injuries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Autograft is to engraft healthy autologous tissue from an uninjured site, reducing immunological rejection and transmission problems compared with allograft and xenograft [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib6)]. Apart from the success of autograft transplantation, almost 10% of these procedures fail due to infection and necrosis [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib7),[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib8)]. Moreover, autografts present limitations such as donor morbidity and limited availability for severe injuries [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib6)]. Decellularized ECM tissue has been explored as an alternative. However, these matrices often have lower mechanical properties than those observed in native tissue [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib9)]. Also, limited [vascularization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/vascularization "Learn more about vascularization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of ECM matrices can limit the diffusion of nutrients and oxygen across the matrices and lead to fibrotic tissue deposition [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib10)]. The application of the native ECM in the field of tissue regeneration has been discussed elsewhere [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib12)].

Since current strategies cannot achieve satisfactory results for tissue regeneration, novel strategies are being developed to address current challenges. Several animal studies demonstrated the feasibility of developing a [biomimetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biomimetics "Learn more about biomimetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") scaffold to support the regeneration of damaged tissue [[[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib14), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib16)].

The engineered scaffold should provide appropriate functions for the tissue and restore the tissue with cellular phenotypic expression. Moreover, unlike [bulk](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/volume "Learn more about bulk from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") prostheses, scaffold, under an engineered porosity, counteracts the [foreign body reaction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/foreign-body-reaction "Learn more about foreign body reaction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (FBR), which occurs by the recruitment of M1 macrophages as a consequence of an inflammatory immune response and the possible formation of foreign [body giant cells](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/body-giant-cell "Learn more about body giant cells from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In fact, if the biomaterial is bioinert, it will be surrounded by a fibrotic scar; if it is not, it will be degraded in time, which is not desirable using [replacement prostheses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/prosthetic-replacement "Learn more about replacement prostheses from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib17)]. Differently from bulk prostheses, the tissue [engineering scaffolds](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/engineering-scaffold "Learn more about engineering scaffolds from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are designed to be populated by cells and to degrade in a controlled manner, thus promoting the formation of a functional tissue.

Along with genetic characters, the cell [microenvironment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/microenvironments "Learn more about microenvironment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") inside the body determines cells’ function. The insight about the role of the microenvironment is essential to design and fabricate a scaffold mimicking the native ECM. The main function of the scaffold is to enable cell growth in the third dimension (3D), which is relevant in all tissues and organs. There are different approaches to fabricating a 3D [biomimetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biomimetics "Learn more about biomimetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") construct. In the first approach, the scaffold seeded with cells provides a [template](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/dna-template "Learn more about template from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to direct host cells to create their own ECM in vitro _,_ which can be performed in static or dynamic (i.e., in a bioreactor) culture conditions before being implanted [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib18),[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib19)]. In another approach, the acellular scaffold is fabricated as a [medical device](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/medical-device "Learn more about medical device from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and implanted into the body, and then the living cells will be recruited from the body after implantation, which is called in vivo approach [[[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib20), [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib21), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib22)]. To engineer a functional scaffold that mimics the characteristic of native tissue, some essential physical or biological factors need to be incorporated into the scaffold to create a healing environment. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#fig1) shows a schematic illustration of the essential factors in the design and development of tissue engineering substitutes. The bioreactors can provide a controlled chemical, electrical, or mechanical environment for cell-seeded scaffolds or cells alone to grow and undergo the remodeling phase for [in vitro studies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/in-vitro-study "Learn more about in vitro studies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or before implantation to enhance the characteristics of the construct and achieve success after implantation.

![Image 11: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2468519424001228-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (783KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2468519424001228-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (783KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2468519424001228-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Schematic illustration of the essential factors in the design and development of tissue engineering substitutes.

Designing scaffolds that can regulate cell behavior, including proliferation and differentiation, is a crucial aspect of tissue engineering [scaffold fabrication](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/scaffold-fabrication "Learn more about scaffold fabrication from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This review paper emphasizes the critical role of stimuli delivered by scaffolds to cells and focuses on recent advances in the development of artificial [scaffolds for tissue engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/scaffold-for-tissue-engineering "Learn more about scaffolds for tissue engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). By highlighting these key developments, this review underscores the importance of [scaffold design](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/scaffold-design "Learn more about scaffold design from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in promoting optimal cellular behavior and advancing the field of tissue engineering.

2. Biomaterials
---------------

Engineered scaffolds must possess suitable physical properties, [biodegradability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biodegradability "Learn more about biodegradability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [biocompatibility](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/biocompatibility "Learn more about biocompatibility from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to function as a [biomimetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/biomimetics "Learn more about biomimetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") substrate for [cell proliferation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/cell-proliferation "Learn more about cell proliferation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), differentiation, and migration. The scaffold's degradation behavior determines the [biochemical](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biochemicals "Learn more about biochemical from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") factors released during the [degradation process](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/degradation-process "Learn more about degradation process from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which can impact [tissue regeneration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/tissue-regeneration "Learn more about tissue regeneration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and inflammatory responses post-implantation. The scaffold's performance in supporting [tissue regeneration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/tissue-regeneration "Learn more about tissue regeneration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is dependent on its [biomechanical properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biomechanical-property "Learn more about biomechanical properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") during this phase. Several studies investigated appropriate biomaterials that can provide mechanical, biological, topographical, and morphological properties to stimulate [tissue regeneration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/tissue-engineering "Learn more about tissue regeneration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib1),[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib23),[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib24)]. The efficacy of an enormous variety of natural, synthetic, and [hybrid biomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hybrid-biomaterials "Learn more about hybrid biomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has been demonstrated for tissue regeneration [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib23),[[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib25), [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib26), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib27), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib28), [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib29), [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib30), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib31), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib32)]. [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#fig2) shows the advantages and disadvantages of different biomaterials for tissue engineering.

![Image 12: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2468519424001228-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (794KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2468519424001228-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (794KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2468519424001228-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. The most common natural and synthetic biomaterials and their advantages/disadvantages for tissue regenerative engineering, the [synthetic polymers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/synthetic-polymer "Learn more about synthetic polymers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") include Poly(glycolic acid) (PGA), Poly(lactic acid) (poly (d-lactic acid) PDLA, Poly (l -lactic acid) PLLA), Polycaprolactone (PCL), and Poly Lactic-_co_-Glycolic Acid (PLGA) [[[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib41), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib42), [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib43), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib44), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib45), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib46), [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib47), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib48), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib49), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib50), [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib51), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib52), [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib53), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib54), [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib55), [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib56), [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib57)].

As the main component of [ECM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/extracellular-matrix "Learn more about ECM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (i.e., about 25% of total body protein in vertebrates), collagen has been the most popular biomaterial to be explored in tissue engineering. The majority of medical-grade collagen and gelatin, a denaturized form of collagen, are from animal origin, namely bovine and porcine tissue from controlled origins. Human collagen is obtained by biotechnology, isolated from human tissues (e.g., lung and placenta), or from [human cell culture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/human-cell-culture "Learn more about human cell culture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib33)]. In addition, collagen can be modified and crosslinked to control biodegradation. Originally, collagen-based biomaterials have been extensively investigated for skin regeneration [[51](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib34)]. There are different types of collagen-based materials for [wound healing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/wound-healing "Learn more about wound healing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that have been translated into [clinical trials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/clinical-trial "Learn more about clinical trials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as injectable Woun’Dres [Collagen Hydrogel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/collagen-hydrogel "Learn more about Collagen Hydrogel from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib35)], [collagen sponges](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/collagen-sponge "Learn more about collagen sponges from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[53](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib36)], Matriderm [[54](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib37)], DenovoSkin, Transcyte [[55](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib38)], and Engineered Skin Substitute [[56](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468519424001228#bib39)]. Boyce et al. reported the efficacy of collagen-glycosaminoglycan-based scaffolds seeded with autologous [keratinocytes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/keratinocyte "Learn more about keratinocytes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and fibroblasts to reduce the need for donor skin and decrease mortality in patients with more than 50% burn of the total body [surface area](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/surface-area "Learn more about surface area from ScienceDi

# Biomaterials for Tissue Engineering Applications and Current Updates in the Field: A Comprehensive Review

### Affiliations

# Biomaterials for Tissue Engineering Applications and Current Updates in the Field: A Comprehensive Review

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

Tissue engineering has emerged as an interesting field nowadays; it focuses on accelerating the auto-healing mechanism of tissues rather than organ transplantation. It involves implanting an In Vitro cultured initiative tissue or a scaffold loaded with tissue regenerating ingredients at the damaged area. Both techniques are based on the use of biodegradable, biocompatible polymers as scaffolding materials which are either derived from natural (e.g. alginates, celluloses, and zein) or synthetic sources (e.g. PLGA, PCL, and PLA). This review discusses in detail the recent applications of different biomaterials in tissue engineering highlighting the targeted tissues besides the in vitro and in vivo key findings. As well, smart biomaterials (e.g. chitosan) are fascinating candidates in the field as they are capable of elucidating a chemical or physical transformation as response to external stimuli (e.g. temperature, pH, magnetic or electric fields). Recent trends in tissue engineering are summarized in this review highlighting the use of stem cells, 3D printing techniques, and the most recent 4D printing approach which relies on the use of smart biomaterials to produce a dynamic scaffold resembling the natural tissue. Furthermore, the application of advanced tissue engineering techniques provides hope for the researchers to recognize COVID-19/host interaction, also, it presents a promising solution to rejuvenate the destroyed lung tissues.

**Keywords:**
3D printing; Bioactive mineral fillers; Biomaterials; COVID-19; Smart polymers; Tissue engineering.

# [Lahann Lab](/)

![Lahann Lab](//images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5d715b88964ed90001b4ef42/1567790813662-RPI5CG3A0I6NTIJB4JRS/Logo.png?format=750w)

# **Advanced Biomaterials for Tissue Engineering**

As defined by Langer and Vacanti, tissue engineering is “an interdisciplinary field which applies the principles of engineering and life sciences toward the development of biological substitutes that restore, maintain, or improve tissue function”. Major challenges in the field of tissue engineering is not only the deciphering of the biology of tissue formations but also the translating the gained knowledge for the engineering of biomaterials that could reproduce the physiological cellular behaviors. Such feat would offer tremendous opportunities in advancing the field of *in-vitro* disease modeling and therapeutics development.

In cardiac tissue engineering, for example, tremendous effort has been focused on regenerating cardiac tissues that recapitulate *in-vivo* phenotypes of healthy and diseased myocardium. The main motivation stems from the scarcity of human tissue samples as well as the poor regenerative capacity of the human heart that plagues the development of efficacious treatment and therapeutics for cardiovascular diseases when compared to all other human organs. The cost of successfully developing a clinical drug is estimated to be between $800 million and $2.2 billion, and such progress is often challenged by inadequate 2D/synthetic culture models that lack the necessary cellular phenotypes as predictive drug assays. Such challenge necessitates **a collective effort to engineer biomaterials that could harness the biochemical and biophysical properties of *in-vivo* extracellular matrix (ECM).**

**In Lahann Lab**, we employ hydrodynamically-induced fibrillogenesis of fibronectin to fabricate and engineer protein substrates for various tissue regenerations, and investigate various way to incorporate extracellular matrix proteins to model the *in-vivo* characteristics of cellular environment.

***Current projects:***

Cardiac Tissue Engineering Approach to *In-vitro* Modeling of Arrhythmia & Adipocyte Infiltration ([**Do Hoon Kim**](https://www.lahannlab.com/do-hoon-chris-kim))

Scale-up production of fibronectin matrix for the expansion of primary cancer cells for the applications of immunotherapeutics production (**Seongjun Moon, Dylan Neale, Do Hoon Kim**)

Controlled synthesis of protein-glycan conjugate for bioengineering applications (**Dylan Neale)**

Design parameters for engineered ECM

Composition

Stiffness/viscoelasticity

Porosity

Dimensionality

Fibronectin (FN) matrix as a tissue substrate

enriched_points.expanded_content: Advanced biomaterials include natural (e.g., collagen, chitosan), synthetic (e.g., PLGA, PCL), and hybrid materials designed for specific tissue engineering applications.
These properties, such as biodegradability, biocompatibility, and mechanical strength, significantly influence cell proliferation, differentiation, and overall tissue regeneration efficacy.
Innovative biomaterials can modulate cell behavior through biochemical signals and physical cues, promoting desired outcomes like enhanced growth and tissue formation.
Advanced biomaterials are crucial in creating scaffolds for tissue types including bone, cartilage, and skin, aiding in regeneration and repair processes.
Emerging trends like 3D and 4D printing with smart biomaterials aim to develop dynamic scaffolds that mimic native tissue environments, enhancing regenerative outcomes.
